The University question and matters· intimately re-
lated to it constitute undoubtedly the question of the 
hour. It is well that it is so. 
The interest in it has 
been considerably in9reased by the admirable fighting 
speech delivered last Sunday at the North Richmond 
Christian Schools, by His ·Grace the Archbishop of 
Dublin. 
The speech struck the Irish Timea dumb on 
Monday; it goaded the Orange Ezpres, !nto a fury, and 
it is hard to say whether the one fact 1s more satisfac-
tory than the other; both factll are so palpably whole-
some. 
The University of the medical school; the home 
of culture from whence some students not long ago 
Rred mud at a Dublin Lord llayor, .has succeeded, to 
its own discomfiture, in attracting inquiring eyes upon 
_it ; i'ts " prestige " is being weighed up and l?eople are 
naturn.lly ' discovering that the specific gravity of a 
bubble is not considerable. 
His GTace in the course of his 11peech said : -" That 
view is, that if we abstract from the various special 
advantages, including such things as the Oollege Park 
and so forth, which, as a matter of course, a great , 
Stato-enclowed University has to offer to its students, 
and if we look simply to what most people of a practical 
turn of mind would look for, let us say, in one of the 
smaller Universities of Germany, the · advantages of 
sound and scholarly instruction, combined with the 
advantages of such academic associations as are not al-
together beyond the Teach of a well-organised Uni-
versity College of even moderate dimensions, it is by Q.O 
means clear that, as between Trinity College on the one 
hand, and our University College in Stephen's green 
on the other, the balance of advantage is not rather 
upon our side than upon theirs (applause). 
For my 
part, I am strongly inclined to believe that it is. " i 
Heretofore there has been, and even at the presenii 
time there is, too great a half-slave tendency to belittle 
our own and magnify anything connected with the 
bigoted Ascendancy. 
His Grace's views as expressed 
in the quotation we have just made will help to correct 
that unfortunate tendency in connection with the popu-
lar estimate of the Parochial University. 
His Grace devoted a considerable portion of his 
speech to the now " famous '' medical schoot of Trinity 
College. At the wind up of that portion of his speech, 
he said : -" The one sure conclusion from it all is that 
the " brand " of Trinity College in the matter of its 
medical degree conveys no great security that even a 
respectable degree of mediocrity has been reached by 
the man upon whom it is conferred. 
One would think 
that the medical graduates of the University-the really 
competent men amongst them-would have long since 
risen up in protest 'against· the continuance of the 
system under which the conferring of medical degrees in 
so extraordinary a fashion is possible. 
The strength 
of any chain is but the strength of its weakest link 
(hear, hear). 
The standard indicated by the degree of 
any University is the standard reached by the least 
qualified candidate upon whom that degree is conferred 
(applause.) 
This, I submit, is a view of the case de-
serving of the serious consideration of every medical 
graduate of the University of Dublin. But as to what 
those graduates may think or may do, that is their own 
affair (applause). 
What we, members of the outside 
public, have to bear in mind is that there is, as I have 
said from the beginning, just one corner of this work of 
Trinity Oollege upon which some gleam of the light of 
• day has been let in, through the official reports of the 
one independent authority that is empowered by Par-
. liament to lift even a corner of the veil. 
Trinity Col-
lege has only itself to blame if we outsiders come. to the 
conclusion that the system of branding one's· own her-
rings, at all events as the system is practised in that 
establishment, is one that must fail to command the 
confidence of the people· of Ireland.',' 
The Irish Times of Monday, with a prudence that 
suggests a heavy heart if not a wise head, has not as 
much as an editorial line on His Grace's speech. 
But 
the Orange Express, ·from the offices of which Buff 
emerges in the evening, was driven into a fury by the 
Archbishop. 
AfJ its placard contained the oaption 
" Savage Attack on Trinity College," we purchased a 
copy of .A.rdilaun's true blue bigot paper. 
Here is how 
the leadina article in the Orange_ Express commences 
" Tlie Most Rev. Dr. 1W alsh selected the occasion of a 
Sunday gathering held to celebrate the close of the 
summer term at the Christian Brothers' Schools yester-
day to deliver himself of one of the most venemous and 
uncalled-for attacks on Trinity College that !].,~-Ye ever 
been publicly made by any members of the Hierarchy 
to which he belongs.'' 
The phrase " venemous and un-
called for " almost rises to the " surpliced ruffian "

level of controversy. 
His Grace, however, knows well 
that the measure of the coarse abuse thrown at him by 
the Orange · Express will be a fair indication of the 
measure of approbation by which his speech has been 
1·eceived by" tlie sorely • tried " Idolators " of Ireland. 
The Express in its fury harks back to almost twenty 
years ago, and quotes a speech delivered by His Grace 
in_ January? 1886, with a view to attempt to show that 
His Grace m 1905 would not be content with a proper 
endowment for higher education suitable for Catholics 
unless the College to be endowed stood on the very site 
-of Trinity College ! The Express lost a great chance 
!1ere of tickling the bigotry of its readers by not snggest-
mg that the .A.rchbishop in his heart would be • only 
satisfied if the foundations of the wished-for College 
should be cut thl'ongh the smoking ashes of the ruins of 
Trinity! The ~an, Coote, Justice of Peace of Tyrone, 
would probably have bought a dozen copies of the 
Express if that had been in it! 
The infuriated, E:1;press says:-" Struck, apparently, 
with alarm lest his imprecations on the ' Protestant 
atmosphere' of the University should fail to deter mem-
bers of his flock from availing themselves of some of the 
increased facilities lately offered to them at entrance, 
the Archbishop has determined to add a· new -string 
to his sacerdotal bow, and stigmatise the secular teach-
ing given in the College as a fraud." 
Perhaps the 
members of the General Medical Council who reported 
-on Trinity's "famous " medical school, are all Jesuits 
in disguise, and mayhap their remarks on that ancient 
school should not have been called a Report but a "new 
string to a sacerdotal bow." 
His Grace will be comforted somewhat to know that · 
acording to the gentlemanly organ of 'the Or.angemen 
of Dublin, his speech was not so vulgar as that of .the 
Most Rev. Dr. O'Dwyer, even though in the qu;i,lities 
of " wildness and unfairness " His Grace appears to take 
first place. 
The Express says:-" The attack is almost 
on a par with that which Dr. O'Dwyer, the Roman 
Catholic Bishop of Limerick, made at a .. prize distri,, 
bution in his diocese last Tuesday on some of the new; 
entrance scholarships established by Trinity College. 
In vulgarity of tone, indeed, Bishop O'Dwyer's lan-
guage seems certainly to have exceeded anything that 
was said yesterday by Archbishop Walsh, but it would 
have been almost impossible to outdo the Archbisho.p in 
the wildness and unfairness of his language in a matter 
affecting the interests of professional men." 
We wonder when will the libeller of Canon Mciner-
ney pluck up courage to say something. 
Perhaps in 
default of saying anything itself, it would insert a 
le_tter on the matter by some bogus " Roman Catholic 
Farmer!" 
The Irish Times having "kept saying nothing" for 
a whole day was evidently emboldened by the letter of 
Dr. Traill to say something in its issue of Tuesday. 
The libeller of Canon Mcinerney is very mild ; there is 
a complete absence of its bogus " Roman Catholic 
Farmer" form. 
The sound and fury of the shrieking 
Orange Express finds no echo in the apologetic little 
leading article in Alf Fox. 
The Irish Times would 
screech at the Archbishop if it dared; it pays a poor 
compliment to His 'Grace if it hopes that what we ~ay 
call it.s crocodile compliment will weaken the resol1:t10n 
of Dr. Walsh in his strenuous campaign for educational 
'justice. 
Tbe· Irish Times commences its little article 
with the following whisper: -"We print to-d3:y a letter 
in which the Provost of T'rinity College rephes to the 
charges advanced against the Medical School of Trinity 
College on Sunday by Archbishop Walsh. 
Dr. Traill 
pays a handsome compliment to Archbishop Walsh's 
fairness in controversies affecting educational matters, 
and he expresses the conviction that had His Grace 
been aware of the facts now submitted to the public he 
would not have made his attack. 
The Provost takes 
up DT. Walsh point by point, and shows that the state 
of the Medical School is· not such as to deserve the cen-
sure that has been passed upon it. 
Nobody will claim 
that Trinity College any more than any ?t~er human 
institution is perfect, but such an adm1s~1on by no 
means implies that it is not honestly and smcerely .de-
sirous of fulfllling the great trust that long has been 
committed to its charge." 
' 
In marked contrast with the fury of the Orange Ex-
vress' s screeches is the remark of t~e Irish 'l'imes that 
"Dr. Walsh, without doubt, felt 1mpelle~ ~y a sense 
of duty to refer to the Medical School of Tnmty College 
as he has done." 
As a matter of fact Dr. Traill's letter is an utterly 
weak and faulty production. 
The first " statement" 
which Dr. 'l'raill challenges was' not, as a matter of 
fact ever made by Dr. Walsh. 
Whether there is any 
thi1;g, be it much or little, in Dr. Traill's second poi?t, 
it is not of any great importance anyway. 
Regar~mg 
the third matter raised by the Provost of the Umver-
sity of the " famous " medical school, his statement of 
it is confused and at variance with the Archbishop's 
remarks as re1jorted in the Freeman; h_is reply is con-
sequently askew and ineffective. 
The weak ill-informed 
letter of Dr. Traill succeeds in making no material im-
pression on the indictment of Trinity College by His 
Grace on Sunday. 
Perhaps if Dr. Traill reads a ver-
batim report of Di·. Walsh's remarks he will conclude 
that it would have been better ·even for Trinity o'f the 
" famous " medical school if he had not rushed into 
print so quickly. 
His letter may convince Mr. Coote, 
J.P., of Tyrone,~but it will not rmpress the intelligent 
Irish public. 
His Grace may decide that the letter of 
Dr. Traill is not worth noticing_; if he decides other-
wise and replies to it, it will merely mean that the 
lantern will be kept upon Trinity for a further period. 
The more light the better. 
Let us have the truth 
though the sky or even the ''. prestige " of Trinity 
College, should fall. 
The Most Rev. Dr. O'Dwyer made some scathing com-
ments on the Catholic members of the" National Educa-
tion Board " that ought to prick the consciences of these 
gentlemen. 
lJ.?. the course of •his 1:ecent speech his -lord-
ship said : -"d t is s'aid that the whole code of new rules 
was passed by the National Board without one word of 
discussion; and what is amazing, and as one reads their 
names bewildering-without one word of protest by the 
Catholic members. Yet they had warning enough to put 
them on their guard. Now they knew pretty well the 
feelings of the Catholics of Ireland. 
The. whole body of 
the managers-most of whom are Parish Priests-the 
united Hierarchy of Ireland, who on a purely moral issue 
have some right to be heard-have condemned this system 
of :i,malgamation of schools ; yet we do not hear of any 
action by the Catholic Commissioners in the interest of 
Catholic morals. 
Rules 186 and 194 (b) still stand, as if 
no one had condemned them." Having quoted a judg-
ment of _the Holy S~e on the question of mixed schools in 
the Umted States, Dr. O'Dwyer said:-" That is the 
very ~hing. that we, Irish Bishops, object to. It is no 
peculiar view of ours. 
It is in,e:rained in Catholic 
princ~ples, and it is a cruel wrong for any Board in 
Dublin to attempt to coerce our consciences in a matter 
of the kin_d. 
How the Catholics on the Board can de-
fend their ~cti?n-how men who are selected to repre-
sent Catholic mte1:e~ts, can join with the avowed op-
po~ent~ of our rehg1on to force on us a school system 
which 1s ab~orrent to all Catholic feeling passes my 
co11;1-prehens1on." 
These are v1;1ry strong indictments 
agamst_ the Catholic members of the Irish National 
Education Board. 
Who are these Catholic gentlemen 
and what explanation 
have 
they to 
offer to 
Dr'. 
O'I?wy_er. and Irish Catholic 
opinion generally

bas he to say to Dr . . O'Dwyer's words:-" It_ is said

Wha.t has he I to say for himself Y At the last confer-

J.P._; the notorious Mr. Starkie, another "Trinity" Ca-
tholic, and the Right Hon. Lord Killanin, yet another 
"Trinity" Catholic, are also on the Board of "National"

The Irish Times, the libeller of Canon M'Inerney, is 
"righteously" 
indignant at the 
Most Rev. 
Dr. 
O'Dwyer, Bishop of Limerick. 
Here is how this pious 
anti-Catholic and anti-Irish paper commences its lead-
ing article of June 29th:-" From one point of view 
the speech made on Tuesday by Dr. O'Dwyer, the Ro-
man Catholic Bishop of Limerick, is much to be re-
gretted. It is melancholy to find an educated person 
of his position repeating in the twentieth century the 
same vulgar and foolish accusations which were cur-
rent in the early> part of the nineteenth. 
To talk of 
Sir John Nutting's offer to Trinity College as 'offensive 
and absurd,' and as ' insolent, undisguised souperism,' 
can do no good to anyone, and least of all to the Ohurch 
of which Dr. O'Dwyer is an ornament." We feel sure 
that poor Dr .. o :Dwyer will writhe under the high 
moral scorn of such an exalted organ as Alf Fox. 
Further down in the article the cruel libeller of 
Canon M'Inerney says : -" Still we cannot help ex-
pressing our regret that the accusations should ha.ye 
been made by a prelate in many ways so enlightened 
as Dr. O'Dwyer, and should have been .couched in lan-
guage which we can only describe as, simply deplor-
able." What an honour· it ,.is to the Catholic world 
that one of our "surpliced ruffians," ,vho happens to 
be a bishop, is referred to as "a prelate in many ways 
so enlightened " by the paper of the imported editor. 
The Wexford University meeting did not adopt the 
capture of Trinity policy. 
And beyond that negative 
result it was like other University meetings; and ex-
cept in the way of keeping the question agitated did 
not add materially to the controversy. 
We think that 
D;r. M'Donald's bold speech did more to precipitate 
the public concerned into· action than a whole year's 
resolution and talk campaigning. 
To go into Trinity 
is • an easy matter; 
and now that a distinguished 
thinker has boldly placed that as a policy before the 
consideration of the country, those many who are re-
solutely agawt any such policy find it imperative 0;1 . 
them to be resolutely for some alternative. 
There 1s 
one thing that all men can con!rratulate themselves_ 
upon, anyway, 2.nd that is that as a result of the in-
creasing intensity of the University agitation, the Ca-
tholic bfacklegs in " Trinity '' are in for a nasty _time 
of it. 
·sir Thomas Esmonde, in the course of his speech at 
Wexford, said:-" Now, as to the second-that of Par-
liamentary agitation, and with that, indeed he would 
like to join the policy of ' Self-Help,' and to speak his 
own views, he would be in favour of amalgamating the 
two in one--Parliamentary ag~tation in Westminster 
and self-help at home." 
So far, so good; but in addi-
tion to agitation in Westminster and self-help at home, 
there is a wide field for real down-right agitation at 
home-agitation that means something; there is also 
room for plenty of practical, organised· self-defence· at 
home. 
We note that Sir Thomas Esmonde referred to the 
possibility of the Local Government Act being used 
with · a, view to helping· the scholarship scheme recently 
established I:,y the Irish Hierarchy. 
It doep not appear from the Press reports we have 
read whether or not the resolutions proposed were 
passed. 
The resolutions proposed consisted of a protest 
against the wrong; a call to the Irjsh Party to take up 
the question in a special manner, and to the country 
to bring the matter home to the minds of the people ; 
commending the Bishops' Scholarships, and suggesting 
that the Committee of the University 
Scholarships 
Fund call a National Conference. 
Mr. Dillon spoke 
against the calling of a Conference on the gro_und that 
the time for such a Conference was not yet ripe; and 
the Press reports do not tell us whetp.er the resolution 
suggesting such a Conference was passed or not. 
Now that public opinion is developing against the Ca-: 
tholic blacklegs ·of " Trinity," would it not be well i'f 
those English-educated youth-" The Freebooters '(_ 
were treated as they ought to be treated by young men 
educated in Colleges in Ireland. 
" The Freebooters " 
a.re a mani'fesfation of that r eo1nm dry rot that has 
so long thwarted anything like a business-like agitation 
on the University Question. It were about time that 
these footballers looked in vain even amongst the stu-
dents of the West British Catholic Colleges for a team 
to play them. We pointed out last week that a foot-
ball team from 
Castleknock 
played these 
" Fr.ee-
booters." Even Castleknock might be expected to have 
more spirit than to play with such people. 
We · are asked to publish the following resolution 
which was passed at the last general meeting of the 
Dublin Branch of the Bookbinders' 
and Machine 
Rulers' Consolidated Union :- " Resolved-That we de-
plore the action of Irish Catholic publishers in allowing 
their names to be printed on the title-pages of prayer-
books of foreign production, and also resorting to the 
mean tactics of effacing the imprint (made in Germany) 
by either scratching it out or pasting a slip over it, so as. 
to lead the public to believe that they were home-made, 
and therefore we take this opportunity of warning the 
people of Dublin and throughout the country of this 
matter, which is very little short of a public scandal." 
T'he I rish Rosary for July is a very interesting num-
ber. Father Ambrose Ooleman continues bis articles 
on Japan which country he has so recently visited. The 
article in the current issue is well worth reading, and 
the general comparison between Japan and Ireland 
make it all the more interesting. 
The population of 
J apan is enormous in relation to the land under culti-
vation. As Father Coleman says, it is the most con-
gested district in the world. "To bring the case home 
to ourselves, it is just as if the whole population of 
England and Scotland had to live here in Ireland, and 
obtain their whole food supply from the area of land 
now under pasture and crops, no food being imported 
from America or any other country." • 
We print in another column the programme of ar-
rangements of the Munster Feis. It will be seen that 
the programme will cover five days. 
The Feis will 
open on Sunday, August 6th, with the Bardic Session. 
On ,Monday the Munster Conference will be held. 
Tuesday and 'Wednesday will be devoted to competi-
tions. 
The proceedings will conclude on Thursday with 
a grand Seilg. 
Intending competitors have up to, 
Saturday, July 16th to ·send in their entries. 
Mr. Ill. A. Foley's is a name familiar to Irish Ire-
landers. 
Mr .. F'oley, whose fust connection with t.he 
movement dates back to a time when the language was 
not in the esteem in which it is held now, is a fluent 
native speaker and writer of • Irish, and one of the 
earnest workers to whom the Keating Branch owes its 
foundation and success. 
In business life he has been 
for ten years salesman and manager on the staff of 
the Remington Typewriter Co., from which he has now 
resigned in order to start for himself. 
We are glad to 
learn from a circular issued by him that, seeing the 
lack of a general typewriter shop in Dublin for office 
~upplies, such as ,carbons, typewriter ribbons, duplicator

Tequisites, etc., and where writing machines of all
makes might be repaired quickly and at reasonable
Pprices, he has just opened such a business at 25 Bache-
lors Walk, a few doors from O’Connell Bridge. His new
enterprise will also include a copying department. The
Irish Treland friends of Mr. Foley will echo our good
wishes for the success which his keen business qualities
lead us to predict for him.

In connection with the Irish Industrial Exhibition
which will be held in the Rotunda in connection with
tl.xe Oireachtas, intending exhibitors from Irish-speaking
districts are requested to send in their applications for
space at once. Entries close on July 14th, and those
organizing the Exhibition—which it will be remembered
was a great success last year—fear that unless possible
exhibitors from the Irish-speaking districts—bthose whom
the Exhibition is mainly intended to benefit—hurry up
they may be left out in the cold. Already we are in-
formed a large number of applications has been received
from non-Irish-speaking centres.

‘We take the following from the Belfast News Letter of
June 26th :—Postal Assistant, competent, take entire
charge Sub-Office, North; sounder instrument. Apply,
stating age, religion, salary expected, references, E. K.,
17455, this office. Will Mr. Walter Long, the Orange-
men’s Chief Secretary, take steps to find out what par-
ticular sub-office in the North requires that applicants
for the situation of Postal Assistant should state their
religion?

We receive a great many queries concerning Irish in-
dustry which we answer through the post. We have a
query now from a manufacturer concerning his difficulty
in obtaining information about the tenders of the vari-
ous public bodies that want goods such as he manu-
factures. This is a matter of great interest to Irish
manufacturers in particular, and to the country in
general. Now the LEADER, though a review covering a
wide range of interests, is regarded rightly as the great
national Irish industrial organ. Manufacturers read it,
and people on the look-out for information concerning
goods made in Ireland consult its advertising pages as
they would consult a book of reference. The difficulty
of our manufacturing correspondent, and of other
manufacturers like him, would be met if the various
public bodies made it their business to advertise all
tenders for goods the like of which are made in Ireland
in the columns of the LEADER, the pioneer and chief
organ of the Industrial Revival. In our advertising
columns those who proposed to buy Irish goods, and
those who had Irish goods to sell, would be brought to-
gether to their mutual benefit and the benefit of the
country. We do not apologise in any way for editorially
making this suggestion merely because we are what is
called “interested parties,’”’ for we are conscious that
our readers know as well as we do that the LEADER is

. the natural and pre-eminently the suitable organ for
such announcements.

CORRESPONDENCE.

Tug Irisn oF MANCHESTER.
Manchester, July 3rd, 1905.

Dear Sir,—A. few years ago you had occasion to hold
up for praise, and very properly so, too, the actions of
thoso connected with a certain concert given by the
children of St. Wilfrid’s, Manchester. Since then the
worthy Irish Canon in charge of the parish has done
many things to forward the Irish movement in Man-
chester ; Irish was introduced into the evening continua-
tion classes, Irish sermons have been given on two occa-
sions during Irish Language Weeks, and the names of

THE YOYAGE OF MAEL DUNE.

About six months ago the Commissioners ordered
that the children of National Schools should be supplied
with story books or books of adventure in addition to
the ordinary readers.

_ Since then may teachers inbued with a spirit of prac-
tical patriotism have eagerly sought after stories pro-
ducfzd in, and relating to, Ireland. For those whose
choice is limited to books written in English the search
has been disappointing, and we know some who have
consequently abstained from introducing any story
roaders; though such abstention may involve a “fair *’
report and consequent loss of increment.

A good beginning in the direction of supplying this
want has now been made by the publication of “ The
Voyage of Mael Dune.”” The translator is Tnuagac
4an Tobaip, and it is a fortunate circumstance that the
task fell to one so well able to do justice to the original,
and to make the tale sufficiently simple and attractive
for c}fulfiren. Of the merit of the story, suffice it to say
that it is doubtful whether it is possible to find another
of the same size so well calculated to stimulate the ima-
ginations of children, and to imbue them with a sense
of their responsibility to their motherland. The book
has been printed in Treland and published by Messrs.
Blackie and Son, and is sold at threepence nett.

WANTED—ORGANISED DEFENCE.

RE the secular Catholic interests of this Catholic
country to be trampled on without as much as one

brave body of men, willing to dare and do, stepping out
from the ranks of apathy and talk, and asking the rea-
son why? “T do not believe,”’ said Cardinal Logue at
the Maynooth Union a few weeks ago, “ certainly in my
memory, that the Government of the country was ever
in such an unsatisfactory state, as far as Catholic in-
terests are concerned, as at the present moment.”” Surely
that pronouncement of Ireland’s grand old Cardinal
should be a call to action, should appeal to whatever of
the verb “to do’’ remains in the rather supine body of
Irish “Idolators.”” Said the Cardinal:—The country
is being governed not only without taking into considera-
tion Catholic views, or the views or interests of the Ca-
tholic majority, it is being governed in open and candid
opposition to them.’”” Are the Papists so poor in spirit
that they are without even the natural instinct to de-
fend themselves? “We have now a Chief Secretary to
the Orangemen of Ireland,”” said his Eminence. Is not
that bitter fact bitter enough to stir a chord of righteous
indignation in the Irish “ Idolators’ ”’ heart? Or has
nature done with her resentments in the tamed Irish
Papist? At the Maynooth Union the Most Rev. Dx.
O’Donnell said : —*“ In public affairs it is not enough to
deserve success.  As the world goes, organised determina-
tion carries further than the merits of a cause.”” Well,
heaven knows, the Irish “ Idolators’’ are badly in need
of “organised determination ’’ and of organised defence.
The Most Rev. Dr. O’Dea, at the Maynooth Union,
said : —“ He believed action was needed. The supreme
intolerance to which they were subjected in this country
was an intolerance which excluded the best intellects of
the country from developing the gifts which God had
given them.”” ‘Well, in the name of all that’s reason-
able, why not action? Ethics have been found sadly

.wanting as an effective force as the side of Catholics’

rights ; the situation is thirsting for a dose of dynamics.

Any time these ten or twenty years the need of an
organisation for the defence of the temporal interests of
the persecuted Catholics was a crying need. All our
national movements are, and rightly so, non-sectarian,
but whilst the Orangemen and Freemasons exist on the
other side, there is no adequate organisation for defence
against them on the Catholic side. The people are so
accustomed to the Freemasons and the Orangemen that
they are taken as matters of course; they are so accus-
tomed, not only to the disorganised state of the Papists,
but the cry about “all creeds, all classes,”” has been so
much on the lips of Papists, that any attempt at organi-
sation by Papists as Papists is shouted at as an act of war
by the Irish T'Wmes and the Wade-Thompsons ; and people
who, as Land Leaguers, appraised at their proper value
the slanders and screeches at Nationalists, and the stories
sent to the Big Brother about outrages and the rest, ap-
pear to get frightened at a blast from the Pervert Priests’

*Protector and people of that ilk. The fact that twe

Protestant bishops are Grand Chaplains, and another
Protestant bishop a Deputy Grand Chaplain to the
Orange Society of which the man, Coote, Justice of the
Peace, is such an ornament, causes no outery. Of
course, no man of understanding is surprised at this state
of affairs. Men of understanding know that once a
societiy for the defence of the interests of “ Idolators ” be-’
came an established fact, it would cease to unduly
oxcite the bigots; the ' Idolators’’ would grow accus-
tomed to the cannonade from the pop-guns of the war-
riors of the Protestant Thousand, and the society would
be accepted on all sides as a matter of course. If the
Cardinal is correct in stating that at the moment “ the
country is being governed, not only without taking inte

consideration Catholic views, or the views or interests of
the Catholic majority, it is being governed in open and
candid opposition to them ’’—if the Cardinal is right in
that, then, surely, the present is most opportune for a
move by way of defence of the legitimate interests of the
people of this country who have the political and econo-
mic misfortune to be “ Idolators.’

The University debacle is fresh in the public mind;
the onslaught of the Ascendancy party on the National
Schools is at present not only in the mind, but on the
nerves of the Irish public; the Orangemen now have Mr.
Long as their special Chief Secretary. A good deal of
this sort of thing is easy work for the Ascendancy, so long.
as their victims, the Irish “ Idolators,”’ are, as “ Idola-
tors,”” an unorganised mob. One would think that no
people had had the lesson of the necessity of organisation
impressed upon them more than had the Irish Papists;
yet, as Papists, they are to a great extent kept from or-
ganising with a view to defend themselves against the
Ascendancy party, the Orangemen, and the Freemasons’
party by some absurd and recreant dread of being called
“bigots *’ by the Irish Times, or being howled at by the
Belfast News Letter and the Daily Express. They have
been too “ tolerant >’ to lift a hand to defend themselves
against intolerance. Many a Papist would fight to the
cannon’s mouth, but he would collapse if the Zrish T'émes
chided him for being a “ bigot.”” What has the  toler-
ance ’’ of the last twelve months availed them? Have
they got a University? Is the National Board con-
siderate of the Catholic conscience? There has certainly
been a considerable amount of give and take, we must
admit. The Papists gave up their arms, and the Ascend-
ancy took full advantage of the situation.

At the Maynooth Union the Most Rev. Dr. O’Dea,
Bishop of Clonfert, said that he believed action was
needed. He said:—“ It was the opinion of many who
held prominent positionsin Ireland that if an association
were established for the defence of Catholic interests,
and adopted a reasonable line of action, that that asso-
ciation should have the sympathy and support of all
honest-minded Irish Catholics.”” The needs and necessi-
ties of the situation have been well and amply discussed
now, and it were time that the verb “to do’’ were
ordered to the front.

Personally the writer would prefer that we should
attempt a complete and final solution of the University

' Question on our own account, renouncing wholly the

policy of “live horse, and you'll get grass.” He has
his fears about any and every solution of the question
that may originate with the British Government. If they
can help it, no University or College that may be estab-
lished by British legislation, will be in the slightest
degree national.

. The nationalism of a University does not depend upon
its politics. The foundation of nationality in an aca-
demic institution of any kind, is essentially national
culture. The nationality of a University is not political
so much as intellectual and spiritual. It draws its in-
spiration not so much from the present as from the
past; from national tradition, rather than from the clash
of politics and parties. Only the diligent cultivation of
native learning in all its branches can make the life,
atmosphere, and genius of a University national.

There is very little chance that native studies will
receive much, if any, attention in any State-founded
University in Ireland ; none at all, if only the founders

can realise their wishes. At the same time I do not
wish to press this point unduly. We are not within
sight of such a University. It is more than doubtful if
any man of this generation will see it established. It
will not be wrung from a foreign Parliament without
agitation very, very different from that which has
hitherto been carried on. If we have grit and deter-
mination enough to carry on such an agitation as will
force the King, Lords and Commons of Great Britain
to grant us a University, we are not likely, without a
strenuous fight, to allow another West-Briton institu-
tion to be set up in our midst. We are already
afflicted with too many of them, public and private,
catholic and protestant, religious and secular, lay and
clerical.

The main point is the urgent necessity of doing some-
thing definite and practical to relieve the present in-
tolerable situation.  This is necessary in the interests
alike of the country and of “students of University
standing. The Ascendancy party have every-
thing. They have not only a practical monopoly of the
educational endowments, which is bad enough, but they
have also, which is far more galling and intolerable, a
monopoly of all the advantages, in every sphere, which
higher education confers. With or without legislation
on the University Question, this state of thing must be
ended.

We are told that it is not to be thought of that an
impoverished people like ours should be asked to estab-
lish a University by voluntary effort. This has became
a catch-cry. It has been heard often enough, at all
events; and at the recent meeting of the Maynooth
Union Cardinal Logue repeated it, although apparently
with some diffidence and misgiving. - Is it anything
better than a catch-cry? Is it a sound argument?
Will it stand the test of close examination and careful
analysis? Not a bit of it. It seems to me that the
man who shows real wisdom in this matter is not the
man who asks, whether impoverished Ireland can afford
to establish a University, but rather he who asks, whe-
ther impoverished, dying Ireland can afford to defer
establishing a University. -

Not long ago Mr. John Dillon, M.P., committed him-
self to the foolish statement that a University founded
and supported by voluntary subscriptions was a thing
absolutely unknown at the present day.  With such
oracles to lead us, it is small wonder that the question
does not progress. At the present moment I have be-
fore my mind six such Universities, and, doubtless, not
very much research would be needed to lead to the dis-

TImpoverished Ireland can much better afford to estab-
lish a University than she can afford to do without one.
She suffers more from the want of a University suited to
her needs than she can possibly suffer from any effort she
may make to provide herself with one. Poor as she is,
she could make no better investment of a considerable
portion of her scanty resources. A University of the
kind that she needs—national, practical, up-to-date—
would do much to minister to her material, national and
higher spiritual well-being. Without such a University
she is moribund, and will so continue.  She is dying,
slowly dying.

The power of catch-cries in Ireland is still quite ap-
palling. Tt has, to some extent, been broken, but it
has not been annihilated.  The readiness with which
random and unsupported statements, like that of Mr.
Dillon, are received by the mass, is a national misfor-
tune. When shall we learn to discount catch-cries, and
to subject unsupported statements to drastic examina-
tion?

History repeats itself. It is repeating itself at the
present moment. The people who constantly asked ten
years ago, how the Gaelic League could possibly hope to
succeed, are now asking just as pertinaciously how an
impoverished people can hope to establish and support
a University without State subvention.  Those who
started the Irish Language Movement had to face such
questions and such questioners. They agitated, and to
some purpose. They forced the recognition of the Irish
language in Irish education.
with agitation, howsoever thorough and success-

hardly be said that the questioners will do just as little
for the University Movement as they did for the Lan-
guage Movement when it most needed help. The one
movement had to make its way without them ; the other
will likewise have to do so.

From every point of view, and altogether apart from:
what the ultimate solution may be, it is a woful mistake
that we have not owrselves attempted something prac-

tical long ago. We do nothing but talk and shout, and

pass idle resolutions, and carry on a spasmodic and un-
real agitation, until action of some kind is forced upon
us from outside We are now going to establish scholar-
ships, but. only as a counter-move to the Trinity College
and Nutting scholarships. Why did we not take some
such action long ago? Such scholarships could just as
easily have heen established years ago as at present.
They would have enabled many a talented young Celt
to secure the higher education which he has been denied.

DR. LAFFAN ON THE UNIVERSITY QUESTION.

Dear Sie—The Clongowes incident, while it has
achieved the one useful result of making it plain that
no one, no matter how highly placed, will intentionally
urge any Catholic to turn his face towards a mixed in-
stitution, yet it will have failed to effect the greater
good if it do not succeed in making Catholics adopt a
more energetic policy towards building up a great Ca-
tholic University. The Jesuits have accomplished won-
ders already by their University College. They were
cabined, cribbed, and confined in every direction, and
yet their success has been so great as, singular to state,
to now constitute one of the greatest obstacles to the
endowment of a Catholic University. For the Dons of
Trinity are not above shop, and they view with alarm
and jealousy the dangerous rival University College, if
properly endowed, would be sure to prove. Why should
Clongowes, that has already done so much for Catholic
education, be allowed to alone found exhibitions to
enable students to go to University College?  Why
should not the whole Catholic body do so, and endow
that College as well? The University Question is one
which concerns both laity and clergy alike. Our
enemies, indeed, tell us that it is a purely clerical one,
while sometimes we meet with members of the latter
body whose attitude towards Catholic University men
conveys the idea that they hold the converse view.
When a fortress is to be taken, a good general will put
forth all his strength and will, I suppose, expel all trai-
tors and weak-kneed soldiers from his standard. Now
what do we propose to do with the recrea.'nts'who
are now pasturing in the various mixed institutions?
Lists of these should be made out, and their parents
should get a month’s notice to clear them out, or to
have themselves left severely alone. The late Father
John Ryan, of New Inn, in that Christian pamphlet of
his, which he published a generation ago, f_urmshed us
with a forcible illustration of the abiding influence of
primitive methods of punishments which might be re-

THE LEADER.

JuLy 8, 1905.

vived with advantage in the case of criminal parents of’
to-day. In his time the offending parents, clafl in the
habiliments of penance, appeared three successive Sun-
days at the portals of their parish church, and after
many toars and much lustration were taken back to the
fold. I can imagine the fits into which the Zrish ZTimes
and Ulster Unionists and the consistent Presbyterians
and fanatical Englishmen would fall if We were men
enough to adopt a course so drastic. Then what about
rival shops within our own domain? TUniversity 'stuA
dents are a limited number in every country. It is of
supreme importance that as many as possible should be
gathered together under one University roof. ‘It sure'ly
would not be too much for us to expect that our Epis-
copal leaders would put an end, once and for all, to
any one of our Catholic Colleges continuing to play at
Universities.  Poor old Dr. Woodlock began this
vicious practice, and it were about time it were ended.
Our people are more fully alive to the necessity for a
great popular University. All they want are leaders—
men who will act, not talk. For fifty years the country
has been flooded with pastorals, lectures, speeches, with
everything but with fight. We know what the result
has been. Let us open a new chapter now; we can
make it too hot for the recreants within our own ranks,
and for the enemies in front of us. If the Bishops
won’t do this, then let them stand aside, and the laity
will tackle it with a vigour that will leave no doubt of
the result. Nor should we stop at the creation of a
great University for ourselves, but we should see that
other people would be compelled to support their Uni-
versities also out of their own resources. The insatiable
English appetite for Irish food would make the with-
drawal of Queen’s College grants and Trinity College
estates a labour of love to our English rulers.—Yours
faithfully,
TuoMAS LAFFAN.
